prompt: For high-volume inline code completion in an IDE, which model choice is usually the best fit?
choices: A reasoning model at every keystroke, so completions are as smart as possible
A small, fast model with low temperature, reserving stronger models for genuinely hard tasks
The largest available model, since bigger is always better
A base (non-instruction-tuned) model, because it just continues text
explanation: Completion is routine and extremely high-volume, so a small fast model at low temperature gives quick, repeatable suggestions cheaply. A reasoning or largest model adds latency and cost with no real benefit here; a base model isn't tuned to follow instructions.

prompt: You want an extraction task to return the same structured result as repeatably as possible. Which decoding setting helps most?
choices: A high temperature, so the model explores more options
A low temperature (and seed where supported), so sampling stays focused and repeatable
A larger max-output-tokens, so the answer is never cut off
Removing the stop sequence so the model decides when to end
explanation: Low temperature makes sampling focused and repeatable — what you want for extraction or code. High temperature adds variety you don't want; max-tokens and stop sequences control length, not run-to-run consistency.

prompt: When does choosing a reasoning model (one that 'thinks' longer before answering) actually pay off? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Diagnosing a subtle, multi-step bug where shallow answers keep missing the cause
Weighing a complex architecture or design trade-off
Generating thousands of routine inline completions per day
Lowercasing a string or formatting a simple field
explanation: Reasoning models earn their extra latency and cost on genuinely hard, multi-step problems like tricky debugging and design trade-offs. For high-volume routine completions or trivial formatting they're pure overhead — a small fast model is the right call.

prompt: Across a toolchain, what single design choice most controls cost and latency without sacrificing quality on the hard cases?
choices: Routing — run a small cheap model on the routine path and escalate only hard or low-confidence cases to a stronger model
Always calling the newest model with its default settings everywhere
Raising temperature globally so answers come back faster
Sending the largest possible context on every call to be safe
explanation: Routing matches each request to the cheapest model that can handle it and escalates only the hard cases, which is the biggest lever on cost and latency across a toolchain. Defaulting to the newest/biggest model or padding context just inflates cost and latency; temperature doesn't control speed.
